title: LA ETS Flavor Model — Listen to an Announcement

Direct_statement.example: The student lounge will be closed...

opening_pattern.rule: 64% start with 'Attention...' — this is the signature announcement opening.

examples: Please ensure your artwork is framed
deadline is this Friday at 5 p.m.
sign up on the club's website

announcement_design.register: Semi-formal. Not casual conversation — this is an ANNOUNCEMENT. Uses: 'We are thrilled to announce', 'Please note', 'Please be aware', 'We want to emphasize'. But NOT stiff bureaucratic — uses contractions occasionally (don't miss, we're offering).
announcement_design.structure_pattern: 1) Greeting/attention-getter → 2) Main news (what) → 3) Details (when/where/how) → 4) Requirements/rules → 5) Call to action or deadline

detail.description: Ask about specific facts stated in the announcement

inference.description: Ask what can be inferred or why something was mentioned

q1_vs_q2_pattern.Q1: Usually main_idea (50%) or detail (43%) — 'What is the purpose?' type
q1_vs_q2_pattern.Q2: Usually detail (50%) or inference (43%) — specific info or inference
q1_vs_q2_pattern.rule: Q1 asks about the BIG PICTURE, Q2 asks about a SPECIFIC DETAIL or INFERENCE. Never both about the same thing.

main_idea: What is the main purpose of this announcement?
detail: What is required in order to...?

option_design.WARNING: 61% of correct answers are the longest option — this is a FLAW in the sample data, not an ETS target. Our generator must avoid this pattern. Target: ≤30%.
option_design.WARNING_2: A is heavily overrepresented (46%). This is likely a bias in the practice material, NOT a real ETS pattern. Our generator must enforce even distribution.

for_detail_questions.description: Uses real words/concepts from announcement but changes a key fact
for_detail_questions.example: Given to first fifty people who apply (wrong: it's a lottery, not first-come)

for_detail_questions.description: Plausible campus action but NOT in this announcement
for_detail_questions.example: Register for university career fair (not mentioned)

for_detail_questions.description: Takes a detail from one part of announcement and applies it to wrong context
for_detail_questions.example: Closed for maintenance starting Friday (wrong entity/time)

for_main_idea_questions.description: Correct about one detail but misses the overall purpose
Plausible announcement purpose but not THIS one
for_main_idea_questions.example: Apologize for delayed renovation (not the purpose)
Encourage students to use the lounge more (it's being CLOSED)

for_inference_questions.description: Goes too far beyond what's stated
Just repeats what's stated, not an inference
for_inference_questions.example: Located adjacent to Lots C and D (this is NOT stated or inferable)

for_inference_questions.description: Valid inference but about wrong topic
for_inference_questions.example: Funded by student ID card fee (no evidence for this)

easy.announcement: Short (40-70 words), single topic, clear structure
easy.Q1: main_idea — purpose is obvious from first sentence
easy.Q2: detail — answer is directly stated with keywords matching

medium.announcement: Medium (80-120 words), 2-3 pieces of info, one rule/requirement
medium.Q1: main_idea or detail with slight paraphrase
medium.Q2: detail requiring attention to specific conditions/exceptions

hard.announcement: Long (100-150 words), multiple details/rules/exceptions, embedded inference
hard.Q1: detail about an exception or condition (NOT the main info)
hard.Q2: inference — 'What can be inferred' or 'Why does the speaker mention'

flavor_markers.target: 60%+ items have a 'must do' or 'please' instruction
Q1 and Q2 test different skills (main_idea vs detail, or detail vs inference)
Uses 'Attention', 'Please note', 'We are pleased' — NOT casual conversation
All distractors are plausible campus announcements/details

anti_patterns: Announcement is too short (<40 words) — not enough info for 2 meaningful questions
Announcement is just a single sentence — real announcements have structure
No specific date/time/location — feels generic, not like a real campus announcement
Both questions ask about the same piece of information
Correct answer for BOTH questions is the same letter
Distractor repeats a phrase from the announcement verbatim
Announcement uses casual conversational tone (Hey guys!, What's up)
Questions can be answered without listening to the announcement (too obvious)
